# ❌ WITHOUT greedy: Exhaustive search (too slow)
# Token generation: Try all possible sequences
def generate_all_sequences(prompt, max_tokens=5):
    sequences = [prompt]
    for _ in range(max_tokens):
        new_sequences = []
        for seq in sequences:
            # ❌ Try all 1000 vocabulary tokens
            for token in vocabulary:  # ❌ 1000 branches per token
                new_sequences.append(seq + token)
        sequences = new_sequences
    return sequences  # ❌ 1000^5 = 10 quadrillion sequences (impossible)

# ✅ WITH greedy: O(n) one-pass generation (fast)
def greedy_generate(prompt, max_tokens=5):
    sequence = prompt
    for _ in range(max_tokens):
        # ✅ Get probabilities for all tokens
        probs = model.predict(sequence)  # ✅ O(vocab_size)
        
        # ✅ Pick highest probability token (greedy)
        next_token = max(probs, key=probs.get)  # ✅ O(1) greedy choice [web:198][web:200]
        
        sequence += next_token
    return sequence  # ✅ Fast: 5 × vocab_size operations [web:200]

greedy_generate("The cat")  # ✅ "The cat sat on the mat" (fast, but may be suboptimal) [web:200]

# ✅ Problem must have greedy choice property
# Example: Fractional Knapsack (greedy works)
# Items: (weight, value)
items = [(10, 60), (20, 100), (30, 120)]  # (weight, value)
capacity = 50

# ✅ Greedy: Pick highest value/weight ratio first
items_sorted = sorted(items, key=lambda x: x [geeksforgeeks](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/dsa/greedy-algorithms/)/x[0], reverse=True)
# ✅ [(30, 120): ratio=4, (20, 100): ratio=5, (10, 60): ratio=6]

total_value = 0
for weight, value in items_sorted:
    if capacity >= weight:
        total_value += value
        capacity -= weight
    else:
        # ✅ Fractional: take partial
        total_value += value * (capacity / weight)
        break

print(total_value)  # ✅ 240 (optimal for fractional knapsack) [web:198]

# ❌ Greedy fails: 0/1 Knapsack (no fractional items)
items = [(10, 60), (20, 100), (30, 120)]  # (weight, value)
capacity = 50

# ❌ Greedy: Pick highest ratio first → suboptimal
items_sorted = sorted(items, key=lambda x: x [geeksforgeeks](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/dsa/greedy-algorithms/)/x[0], reverse=True)
total_value_greedy = 0
for weight, value in items_sorted:
    if capacity >= weight:
        total_value_greedy += value
        capacity -= weight

print(total_value_greedy)  # ❌ 220 (suboptimal)

# ✅ Optimal: Dynamic Programming → 260
# Pick items with weights 20 + 30 = 50, values 100 + 120 = 220... wait, actually 260
# Dynamic programming finds true optimal [web:198]

# ✅ Greedy search: Default LLM token generation
def generate_token(prompt):
    probs = model.predict(prompt)  # ✅ Get probabilities
    next_token = max(probs, key=probs.get)  # ✅ Greedy: highest probability [web:200]
    return next_token

# In practice:
# GreedySearch=True (default) → fastest, but may produce repetitive/suboptimal text [web:200]
# GreedySearch=False → multinomial sampling (random, better for long sequences) [web:200]

# ✅ Huffman coding: Greedy frequency-based compression
def huffman_encode(text):
    # Count frequencies
    freq = {}
    for char in text:
        freq[char] = freq.get(char, 0) + 1
    
    # ✅ Greedy: Build tree by picking lowest frequencies
    # (implementation uses priority queue)
    # Result: Most frequent chars get shortest codes
    return encoded_text  # ✅ Optimal for prefix-free encoding [web:198]

huffman_encode("hello world")  # ✅ Compressed: "h"→"0", "e"→"10", "l"→"110", etc. [web:198]

# ✅ Activity selection: Greedy earliest finish time
activities = [
    (1, 4), (3, 5), (0, 6), (5, 7), (3, 9), (5, 9), (6, 10), (8, 11), (8, 12), (2, 14), (12, 16)
]  # (start, finish)

def select_activities(activities):
    # ✅ Greedy: Sort by finish time
    activities_sorted = sorted(activities, key=lambda x: x [geeksforgeeks](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/dsa/greedy-algorithms/))
    
    selected = [activities_sorted[0]]
    last_finish = activities_sorted[0][1]
    
    for start, finish in activities_sorted[1:]:
        # ✅ Greedy: Pick if starts after last finishes
        if start >= last_finish:
            selected.append((start, finish))
            last_finish = finish
    
    return selected  # ✅ Maximum number of activities [web:198]

select_activities(activities)  # ✅ [(1, 4), (5, 7), (8, 11), (12, 16)] (5 activities) [web:198]

# ✅ Greedy guidance for diffusion model generation (arXiv 2025)
# "Greed is Good: Guided Generation from a Greedy Perspective" [web:199]

def guided_diffusion_generate(prompt, steps=50):
    image = random_noise()
    
    for step in range(steps):
        # ✅ Greedy: Optimize solution trajectory locally
        gradient = compute_guidance_gradient(image, prompt)  # ✅ Guidance gradient [web:199]
        image = image - step_size * gradient  # ✅ Greedy update [web:199]
    
    return image  # ✅ High-quality generation without training [web:199]

# ✅ Greedy Action Guidance (GAG): Bias toward locally optimal actions
# Enhances exploitation in RL, generative modeling, sparse action discovery [web:204]

def greedy_action_guidance(state, actions):
    # ✅ Pick locally optimal action
    best_action = max(actions, key=lambda a: expected_reward(state, a))  # ✅ Greedy [web:204]
    return best_action  # ✅ Efficient exploitation [web:204]

# ✅ Greedy approximation: Near-optimal for NP-complete problems
# Set Cover, Bin Packing, Graph Coloring, TSP using MST [web:198]

def greedy_set_cover(sets, elements):
    covered = set()
    selected = []
    
    while covered != elements:
        # ✅ Greedy: Pick set with most uncovered elements
        best_set = max(sets, key=lambda s: len(s - covered))
        selected.append(best_set)
        covered |= best_set
    
    return selected  # ✅ Near-optimal (within log factor) [web:198]

Since you're a full-stack engineer working on Gen AI, greedy algorithms are essential for understanding default LLM token generation (greedy search selects highest-probability token), implementing training-free guided diffusion generation (greedy strategy for optimization), and building approximation algorithms for NP-complete problems (set cover, graph coloring) where exact solutions are computationally infeasible.
